data.title: Victoria's Secret Is Challenged In The U.S., Stock Price Is More Reasonable
data.description:
Victoria's Secret & Co. (NYSE:VSCO) reported relatively muted Q2 and H1 '25 results.

The company is not growing much in the aggregate, and I have reasons to believe that the U.S. business is doing significantly worse than last year. Margins were ok, but the company is expecting a less profitable H2 '25, given the impact of tariffs.

Since I last wrote about VSCO, with a Hold rating "due to the high expectations already priced in and the potential for rapid sentiment shifts," the stock is down 53%. At current prices (~10/13x guided FY25 earnings), it seems much more reasonable, albeit I remain worried about execution, and maintain a Hold rating.

Q2 '25 results

Last time I wrote about VSCO (after the Q3 '24 report, in late 2024), the company seemed to be stepping out of a cyclical downturn, with comps improving 3%. However, the stock price had become so optimistic that a recovery was not enough, with a complete growth rally already priced. As of H1 '25, the growth rally has not occurred, margins have deteriorated, and the stock has puffed.

Meager growth on easier comps: The company posted comps up 3% in Q2 '25 and 1% in H1 '25. Although this is a regular result, we have to consider that it comes from -3% and -4% respectively in H1 '24, meaning the results are still not above some pretty bad comps from H1 '24. Compared to 2024, the Google Trends are about flat as of late August.

Further, we are building a four-year track record of down comp sales, with FY24 flat versus FY23, FY23 down 9% versus FY22, and FY22 down 8% versus FY21. This is important when considering the potential for turnaround and execution.

International shows value of brand: The U.S. is the main driver of the company's revenues (55% from stores, up to 85% when considering DTC), but I'm not so sure about operating income.

The company does not disclose segment operational income, only revenues, but reading the 10-K, one can notice that the international revenues are mostly franchise revenues and that

revenue recognized under franchise and license arrangements generally consists of royalties earned and recognized upon the sale of merchandise by franchise and license partners to retail customers.

The above means that international revenues are probably almost pure gross margin, because royalties have very little cost.

The good point is that the company is doing very well internationally, with sales increasing 22% for the quarter, and partner-operated stores expected to increase 10% for the year.

Another sign of brand value is the fact that the beauty category (the largest part of partner-managed fleet abroad, and generally licensed) is in its 8th quarter of growth.

Tariff challenges: Despite growth in the most profitable business (by margins, not necessarily in the aggregate), the company's gross margins were flat (+20bps), and are expected to contract in Q3. The company said that this was caused by better full price selling (positive for margins), offset by higher freight and tariffs.

Because of international growth, I would have expected the general gross margins to move higher. The fact that they didn't leads me to believe that U.S. gross margins are actually much more challenged.

SG&A deleverage: On the same vein, if the sales that are almost pure profit (royalties) are growing so much, then it is hard not to leverage SG&A and operating margins. However, the company actually delevered operating margins by 60bps both for the quarter and first half.

What this indicates, again, is that probably operating margins in the U.S. business are doing much worse than on the aggregate, which is lifted by International, but still down.

Lower profitability expected for the year

The company is expecting to have more challenges in H2 '25. It will face worse comps, as H2 '24 was one of growth. It will also face more challenges from tariff impacts on CoGS (or in pricing).

The company expects adjusted operating income of $270/320 million versus $375 million last year, and adjusted FCF of $150/200 million versus $286 million last year. FCF is expected to almost halve versus last year.

Potential interested party

In late May, the company announced that it had adopted a rights program in order to prevent one shareholder from gaining a larger stake in the company. The shareholder has accumulated a mid-teens stake and has stopped buying after the rights offering.

We have seen significant acquisition activity in the consumer discretionary space. The ones that come to mind more easily are Guess? (GES) and Skechers (SKX).

The ones not buying shares are the company's insiders, showing no new money share buys in the last twelve months. All "insider accumulation" has come from share-based payments. Indeed, shareholders have been diluted so far this year.

Valuation is fair but not super attractive

The company expects EPS for the year of $1.8/2.2, which compares to a share price of $22 as a multiple of 10/12x. On an FCF basis, the FCF yield guided for the year is 10% at the midpoint ($180 million versus market cap of $1.8 billion).

These are not high valuation multiples, especially considering the value of the brand. The company is growing outside the U.S., because Victoria's Secret still commands a value in many regions.

In addition, we have two potential tailwinds.

First, the interested party above, which could potentially make an offer for the company, or part of it.

Second, the removal of the de-minimis tariff exemption in the U.S., helping all large apparel companies versus their competitors from China, like Shein and Temu. I do not know how much of the company's market was affected by Shein/Temu competition (given that quality is probably a bigger concern in categories like Beauty and Innerwear). Still, this could prove positive.

We also have two headwinds.

The first is the disposable income impact of tariffs, which could affect sales in the U.S. Related to this, the impact of higher tariff costs on company margins and productivity. This is already evident in H1 '25, if one accounts for the offsetting positive effect from International growth.

The second is the company's management, which has repeatedly failed at execution since the company went public. I have covered the different failures in more detail in previous articles. The company has recently replaced its CEO and CFO, but I'm still uncertain about management and Board quality.

Overall, I think the price is fairish, considering the pros and cons. However, I do not think it is super attractive, because I do not see a path to growth given the challenges in the U.S. and the lack of execution.

For the time being, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Who's In Charge Of The U.S. Economy (If Not The Fed)?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Lyn Alden Schwartzer, founder of Lyn Alden Investment Strategy and contributing analyst to ElliottWaveTrader.net, discusses fiscal dominance - the rising influence of government deficits and spending over central banks on markets and inflation. She explains how this shift creates a hotter, less cyclical economic environment and what it means for investors navigating these changes in 2025.

Transcript

Mike Larson: In the world of fiscal dominance, the rules are different. Economies and markets behave differently. And if you don't change your approach as an investor, it can cost you dearly. That's why I was so pleased to speak to this week's guest. Her top-down macroeconomic analysis is superb, and her comprehensive knowledge of bank lending drivers, technological developments, digital assets, and the intersection of policy and markets is second to none. Her name is Lyn Alden Schwartzer, and she is founder of Lyn Alden Investment Strategy and an analyst at elliottwavetrader.net. Almost 800,000 follow Lyn's work on X. More than 322,000 viewers have watched her recent presentation at Bitcoin 2025 on YouTube. And now Lyn is joining me here on the MoneyShow MoneyMasters podcast.

Lyn, thank you so much for joining me here today. I mean, there's so much to talk about on the macro front. I know a lot of your work is kind of based on that macro overlay. But just this is the first time I've had you on the podcast - obviously, I follow your work, but some of our viewers may not have - why don't you talk a little bit about your background and what it is you do, at this point, then we'll kick off from there.

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Sure. So thanks for having me. My initial background was actually Electrical Engineering, and I then went into System Engineering, Engineering Management. But I've had a lifelong interest in investing. I've been doing that alongside engineering for a long time, and eventually, I left engineering to focus on analysis full time. I take some of a novel approach of using actually kind of systems analysis, in my market analysis. So, basically, when I look at, capital flows and things like that, I treat it like I would a system, with inputs, outputs, governing logic, because these are, at the end of the day, human-designed systems. Obviously, it's very, very complex ones, with a lot of moving pieces. And so, at lynalden.com, I cover equities, commodities, other asset classes, and I'm the author of Broken Money. Came out in 2023, and it catalogs the history of kind of the intersection of technology and money.

Mike Larson: Great. Well, I appreciate that background. My father being a Mechanical Engineer for many years in aerospace at Pratt, when -- he's going to enjoy this segment, I'm sure. Let's talk about one of the big points that you had earlier this year, and that's fiscal dominance. What does that concept mean? What does it mean for markets in the economy? And I know that that was a lot of what you talked about at the beginning of the year. How have things changed or not changed now that we're talking in late August of 2025?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So for the most part, they've not changed. Kind of the whole point of fiscal dominance is, it tends to last for quite a while. And so, basically, what that means is that unlike the past 40-plus years, where monetary dominance was in play, basically the bank lending decisions and by extension, the central bank, tailoring its policy to either encourage more lending or pull back lending, that was monetary dominance. Now we're in more of a fiscal dominance environment, meaning that the size and the direction of the deficits are, generally speaking, larger variable than the central bank and even kind of the broader banking system. Really, the only thing that's changed since then is, obviously, tariffs have heated up recently, and so that's a slight break on the fiscal deficits because you're bringing in more revenue, so far mostly from the American public. And so that kind of offsets the deficits, but that's still fairly small in the grand scheme of things.

We are still running above baseline deficits as a percentage of GDP. In addition, we have more information from the Fed, which is that it looks like they're going to be a little bit more dovish going forward. So while variables have changed, we're still largely in play in terms of fiscal dominance.

Mike Larson: Sure. And I definitely want to talk about the Fed a little bit and what we've just learned in the past week on that front. But let's talk about a fiscally dominated market/economy versus one where monetary policy is the main force. What does that mean for an investor? What do they sort of need to know about those two dynamics?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So on one hand, it tends to run hotter, which is to say that, when you're running a larger deficit, all else being equal, that's somewhat of a stimulus, with the cost often being basically debasement or inflation, at least above target otherwise would be. And it also tends to be less cyclical because a lot of those deficits are not really tied to the business cycle per se. If anything, they increase during a recession. And so while it's still possible to have recession and you still do have cycles, they tend to be somewhat muted. It's kind of like how -- if you look at equities, there are obviously certain equity sectors that are a lot more cyclical than others, whereas some like defense or health care just tend to be on their own cycles compared to these more business cycle ones. And what happens in fiscal dominance is kind of the broader economy tends to look more like those types of industries to some extent because the deficits are kind of always on compared to the more cyclical nature of bank lending.

One way to kind of put it is that over the past 70 plus years, for the majority of the time, net new annual bank loans, and you can even add corporate bonds, so bank loans plus net new bonds issued, that was a larger sum than public deficits. It only kind of in brief recessions do you see that the public deficit would be greater than that kind of private sector lending. Whereas now, we generally see even outside of recessions in recent years, the public deficit is larger than the sum of all new bank loans and corporate bond issuance in a typical year. And so that just -- it's not to say that there's other things that aren't relevant, but they're a smaller share of the pie than they used to be.

Mike Larson: And is this something we're seeing globally, not just in the U.S.? I mean, obviously, China's been sort of on this. You know, we got to do more stimulus. We got to do more stimulus-type approach. Some of the things the administration's been doing seem to be getting places like Europe rethinking the deficit spending. What are your thoughts on that?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So it's more so a U.S. phenomenon than a global phenomenon, but on a country-by-country basis, it's certainly relevant. I think there's a cycle that countries tend to go through. The U.S. currently is running larger deficits as a share of GDP than most European nations. It's part of the structure of the euro itself that makes that a little bit more difficult to run larger sustained deficits. But they all have kind of similar dynamics, which is older demographics. They fuel some of the deficit spending because you have that more top-heavy entitlement system. So whether it's China, whether it's Europe, whether it's US, they all kind of are in that boat together.

And it just so happens that because U.S. health care is very expensive and the way we kind of structured our entitlement spending, as well as, generally speaking, a bigger share on defense as well, we're running hotter than the rest of those. But things matter around the margins. So the fact that Europe's running hotter than is normal for Europe is still relevant from an investing standpoint. It's just an absolute sense the U.S. is really kind of fiscally the most dominant.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, let's shift gears here a little bit. You brought up the Fed, and obviously, we had Jay Powell essentially. I mean, not guaranteed, but strongly suggest the Fed's going to be cutting when September rolls around. There's certainly been plenty of political pressure on the Fed to operate and do more than that. What are your thoughts on what Powell said, what it means, and sort of what the path is forward for the rest of '25 and as we roll into '26?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Yeah. I think he took a slightly dovish take compared to what the market expected, and the market kind of pretty much priced that in as soon as he was speaking about it. And basically, what we should expect is generally at least slightly lower interest rates. He made no commitment to the idea that he's going to keep doing it in 2026, but he did heavily imply that we're at least getting one or two cuts perhaps, upcoming.

One of the challenges - this goes back to fiscal dominance - is their industry tool is mostly around trying to get banks to either lend more or lend less because, historically speaking, during periods where inflation's hotter, there tends to be a lot of money creation from bank lending. So for example, in the '70s, when the baby boomers were entering their home buying years, that was kind of the peak period of U.S. loan formation. There's a lot of loans being done and greater than average money supply growth and the inflation that they came with. Obviously, there are other factors like the oil shortages and things like that where that money supply growth actually does translate into much higher prices because you don't necessarily have that productivity offset. But basically, that bank lending is a really big component of it. And so most of the tools are around that.

The problem is that in this whole kind of COVID and post-COVID environment, because we're in fiscal dominance, the inflation we've seen has not been from excess bank lending. Bank lending rates are pretty muted. If anything, they're kind of historically normal and on the lower side. And so the Fed trying to keep rates high to slow down bank lending is kind of working around the problem. The actual core of the -- what fueled all this was the deficits. Back in -- years ago, it was a stimulus, and now it's a less extreme but more sustained, just ongoing fiscal spending, which tends to run things hot.

And so the Fed kind of find themselves in a position where there's no right answer. If they cut rates, it doesn't look great because they're cutting rates into, like all-time high stocks, gold, bitcoin, low employment. It looks -- it kind of looks dovish on that surface.

On the other hand, the housing market is pretty much at a standstill. I think even with some cuts, it's not really going to change that, and bank lending is not the issue. So I can certainly see why they'd make a case for cutting. And it's just -- the fiscal dominance is more about the fiscal entity than what the central bank's going into.

Mike Larson: Got it. You brought up markets, so I think that's a logical pivot here. I mean, obviously, we've seen precious metals be extremely strong. We've seen bitcoin, as you mentioned, do very well and certainly other cryptos, in the digital asset space as well. What are your thoughts on what's primarily driving it? Is it all the I need safety, purchasing power protection? Is it a lot of the projects going on in the digital space? I mean, how much some -- in terms of driving factors of those kinds of asset classes are similar or versus different in your opinion?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So in the digital asset space, I think basically right now, corporations are the bulk of what's driving it. So if you look at, kind of where sales have come from, it's generally come from those who've held for a while and are up pretty significantly on their gains, and then they either -- they sell to rebalance or to increase their consumption level in some way. Basically, for a lot of people, 100,000 bitcoin was kind of their magic number. You know, some of them have been holding since a 1000 a coin or even less. Some people have been holding since 10,000, but you kind of hit that psychologically important number. You get this ongoing kind of distributive effect.

Another hand, there's -- Strategy has been purchasing. Metaplanet has been purchasing. And then we've seen this kind of breakout in other -- like altcoin treasury companies. I think, like kind of prior cycles, it probably won't end well. A lot of companies filled up with kind of tertiary altcoins. It's probably not something we look back on years from now and say, "yeah that was a good thing to do." But, so I think it's -- that's kind of where that rotation's happening away from kind of older whales and more toward these kind of corporate treasuries until they get satiated for this particular cycle.

Mike Larson: Okay. And while we're on that subject, I mean, obviously, we've seen bitcoin in particular, but some of the other coins get more mainstream money coming in as a result of changing policies on the sort of advisor side, plus the rollout of all these ETFs that make it easy for people who are non-crypto people to invest. Where do you think we are in that trend? I mean, is this still early stages, mid stages? What are your thoughts on that?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: I still think it's pretty early stages. I think that things can get ahead of themselves for periods of time. So for example, when the ETFs came out, there was kind of like a local bubble, and bitcoin kind of end up chopping sideways the next seven months because of it as things had to kind of work itself out. And then, with the election and the rapid change in perception around what the regulation environment would be, that also gave us another really big pop, in bitcoin, which took another kind of seven months or so to absorb before we went on to new highs again.

So while things can get ahead of themselves temporarily, I still think that in the zooming-out sense, they were still pretty early. Most companies don't have bitcoin on their balance sheet, and I think even in the next five years, that'll still be the case. That'll still be a niche thing. Most governments still don't have it on their balance sheet. A handful do in sovereign wealth funds and things like that. And most pensions, they might have a little slice here or there. But I just think that in the grand scheme of how much capital's out there, bitcoin as a $2 trillion asset is still pretty small in the grand scheme of things. Even though basically it's this global money that's still smaller than some individual equities are in terms of market cap. And so I still think it's pretty early in the kind of integration of the digital asset space and then broader traditional markets.

Mike Larson: Got it. I do want to talk about kind of your market outlook. But before I do, obviously, given your engineering background and some of what you said recently on X, you talked about how you use AI for -- in your own business, but there are certain things like summarizing novels and high-priority art projects that it's still not maybe -- the reality hasn't caught up to the hype. I'm just kind of curious as to your perspective, again, as a business person as well as just sort of the fascination with that part of the market, what your thoughts are?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: I mean, directionally, I think we're going similar to full self-driving, which is some of the proponents would always say it's like two years away, and it's been like 10 years of that. And, of course, in some areas, it can work in pretty kind of controlled areas. But in the grand scheme of things, anything in the -- problem is anything in the field is way, way harder. It's always easy to automate things in controlled environments than to automate things where there's always edge cases everywhere. And so, full self-driving has kind of eluded the technical optimists.

And, I was kind of writing that in response to basically seeing people say, in five years, like, it'll displace all of our jobs and things like that. While obviously, there are certain areas that it's very -- it's already eating into. It's pretty ripe to do that because it kind of is those like controlled environments, where it can -- it can kind of iterate, learn, and then just do things with minimal edge cases to worry about.

Anytime you get into the field, so when we talk about like robotics in the field, for example, it's got basically that full self-driving problem on steroids because even the roads themselves are somewhat of a controlled environment, and yet it's still hard, so let alone everything else that might have to be done. So while I think that AI will keep progressing, I think it's already -- it's good enough that it can impact more than it already has. It just takes time to kind of integrate into business and consumer processes.

I generally fade the really kind of hyping super optimistic view or some people view it as a negative thing, but basically, the technical optimist view that these things are just going to be so dramatically impactful compared to humans in five years, I think is -- I think that's too optimistic. I think we have to have more sober outlooks on it.

Mike Larson: Fair enough. When it comes to markets and asset classes, I mean, obviously, equities have had a huge rally. We're close to or at highs depending which index you're looking at. Gold is still sort of kicking around that 3,400 level and bitcoin pulling back between a 110, 115, give or take. Really speaking about those asset classes and maybe if you want to throw the dollar out there too, what are you generally still bullish on? What are you getting more cautious on? And where do you think there might be a reversal out there that we're not speaking about?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So I'm still bullish on bitcoin. Gold, I started worrying about this spring because it got kind of heated. Fortunately, that's been more of a sideways consolidation than a correction. And I think it's kind of burning off some of that heat kind of like bitcoin had to do a couple times this cycle. So I'm still long-term bullish on it, but I -- it's still kind of working through that.

I'm increasingly a little cautious on U.S. equities at these levels, given the ongoing headwinds that are still there, including tariffs and things like that. I also think that rate cuts, while helpful, are probably not going to be as stimulative as people think because without a kind of lower low in rate, especially longer-term rates, I think any sort of like home refinancing cycle is going to be pretty muted this time compared to prior cycles. And so while it helps certain entities and individuals around the margins, I don't think that we'll have any sort of big gains from that rate-cutting cycle.

Where I tend to be more bullish is foreign equities. I still think they're very under-owned. In many cases, it's still fairly inexpensive, and upcoming dovish cycle should be somewhat beneficial to them. So, I tend to think there's a lot of -- kind of returns to be had over the next three to five years for more of that foreign sector.

Mike Larson: Okay. And again, everybody has sort of different policies about this. Any particular stocks, ETFs, sector ETFs that you think are more promising than others that you'd be comfortable naming, or you just want to kind of keep it at that macro level?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: I would say at the macro level, although I would point out that I tend to be interested in some of the financials in those areas. So I've been interested in Latin American financials, for example, basically, things on the more value side of that. And these kind of -- when people talk about like equity rotations, it's always hard. There's been this very like a long line of people calling for rotation over the past 5, 10 years. We've had just kind of, pretty miraculous outperformance of U.S. equities compared to the foreign market.

I think the kind of key thing to keep in mind is, anytime there's a rate cutting cycle, that's kind of like the shields down, where if rotations are going to happen, that's a really good catalyst for it to happen. It ended up not happening during the kind of the pre-COVID rate cutting cycle because we had all these other factors coming in at once. So it's not to say that a cutting cycle does cause a rotation, but when you already have, basically, all this global capital on one side of the boat stuffed into U.S. markets, huge period of outperformance, and then you do get ongoing cutting cycles, that tends to be a useful catalyst.

And really, since the Fed started cutting last year, at least a little bit, we have generally seen an uptick in performance of international markets. In many kind of areas, they've kind of at least stopped underperforming. They haven't had some sort of major positive rotation yet, but at least there is kind of a bottoming if you look at some of these ratio charts. And I think it's useful to keep an eye to see if that sustains. I expect that it will, but I'm kind of fully aware that this has been one of the hardest things to call as any sort of change in leadership.

Mike Larson: Okay. I guess probably the last asset class I haven't talked all that much about would be what to do in the fixed income space. I mean, do you see things in terms of credit quality that concern you? Do you have any thoughts on the yield curve and so on? People are looking to trade those types of trends. Where do you stand on them?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So for, like, five years, I've been a pretty outspoken bond bear. But now that yields are higher, I'm less kind of bearish going forward, even though I'm not particularly much of a bull either. I think that we will get shorter on the front of the curve. I think the longer end of the curve is probably in a choppy sideways pattern. So it's a tradable asset, but it's not particularly compelling from like a longer-term investing standpoint. You get potentially a lot of volatility without a ton of returns from it.

I'm not that worried about credit quality overall. I think there's obviously some pockets that are concerning, but they tend to -- it's a more slow unfolding default cycle, like, for example, commercial real estate and so forth. Those tend to not be big enough to sync other parts of the economy, unlike, for example, the subprime mortgage crisis was -- it was large enough. It hit right at the core of residential housing. I think, fortunately, we're not in that situation.

So these more kind of smaller pockets where, you might have a multi $100 billion problem, which sounds like a big thing, but when you're talking in the size of the U.S. market, with well over like a 100 trillion in assets out there, those tends -- issues tend to be absorbable.

So I don't focus heavily on the -- there are credit analysts that dive a lot deeper than me, but I mainly look out for big things that could affect macro. And I tend to think that they're -- at the current sizes, they're probably more contained to their areas.

Mike Larson: I guess, last question is kind of odd one because surprises by their nature are surprising. Is there anything that you think could be surprising or anything that might be we haven't talked about already that really is something an investor should be keeping their eye on here?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Yeah. It's a good question. I think a still big question - I'm not the first one to raise this - is how long is this CapEx cycle in AI going to go. Basically, the GPU cycle, the data centers, and all that. Because if you look at the broad economy, most pockets are kind of stagnant, not doing amazing, and anything kind of tech- or AI-related is kind of lifting everyone up. And so as soon as that slows, that's another pinch of catalyst for global capital to want to get out. Then you have that inverse wealth effect potentially.

And so, whenever you have a very financialized economy where rising stock prices fuel consumption and then that consumption fuels rising stock prices, the problem is that virtuous cycle works well up on the rising side, but then it can kind of surprise you with a vicious cycle on the other side. And well, I don't think we're there yet. It is something that I do monitor pretty closely, just because, again, there's all this capital already stuffed into U.S. markets, and it doesn't take much to at least get a very kind of slight reversal.

Mike Larson: Okay. Well, Lyn, I want to thank you so much for taking some time out here to join the podcast.

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Thank you.
